coredata.dc:title: Detection and elimination of tetracycline: Constructing multi-mode carbon dots for ultra-sensitive visual assay and CDs/TiO2 for photocatalytic degradation

Song, Yanhua
Wang, Fangke
Li, Jingyao
Zhang, Xiaozhen
Zou, Haifeng
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasensitive detection and degradation of tetracycline (TC) has gained increasing attention owing to their inevitable detriments to human and ecosystem. Many studies have focused on mono-functional detection or degradation. However, the design of multi-functional materials is still a challenge. Till now, a novel protocol applied to the consecutive detection and complete degradation of TC are rarely successfully reported. Herein, we not only developed a multi-mode optical carbon dots (CDs) for ultra-sensitive sensing, but also further manufactured CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst that can degrade TC 100 %, completely eliminating the TC for the first time. The as-prepared CDs can selectively detect TC with low detection limit of 5.2 nM and 10.7 nM within only 40 s, accompanied by wide linear interval of 1.0–50.0 μM and 50.0–250.0 μM, respectively. Therefore, the fabricated point-of-care testing platform can realize “in-situ” visual detection rapidly and efficiently. Furthermore, a highly active CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst was prepared by rationally modifying CDs on TiO2 and exhibited almost 100 % degradation behavior towards TC within 70 min. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching and photocatalysis were lucubrated. Notably, our proposed protocol can drive consecutive detection and elimination towards tetracycline, which powerfully reduces the potential harm and provides a new strategy for the eradication of pollutants.

head.ce:title: Detection and elimination of tetracycline: Constructing multi-mode carbon dots for ultra-sensitive visual assay and CDs/TiO2 for photocatalytic degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasensitive detection and degradation of tetracycline (TC) has gained increasing attention owing to their inevitable detriments to human and ecosystem. Many studies have focused on mono-functional detection or degradation. However, the design of multi-functional materials is still a challenge. Till now, a novel protocol applied to the consecutive detection and complete degradation of TC are rarely successfully reported. Herein, we not only developed a multi-mode optical carbon dots (CDs) for ultra-sensitive sensing, but also further manufactured CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst that can degrade TC 100 %, completely eliminating the TC for the first time. The as-prepared CDs can selectively detect TC with low detection limit of 5.2 nM and 10.7 nM within only 40 s, accompanied by wide linear interval of 1.0–50.0 μM and 50.0–250.0 μM, respectively. Therefore, the fabricated point-of-care testing platform can realize “in-situ” visual detection rapidly and efficiently. Furthermore, a highly active CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst was prepared by rationally modifying CDs on TiO2 and exhibited almost 100 % degradation behavior towards TC within 70 min. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching and photocatalysis were lucubrated. Notably, our proposed protocol can drive consecutive detection and elimination towards tetracycline, which powerfully reduces the potential harm and provides a new strategy for the eradication of pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of zero-dimensional carbon fluorescent nanomaterials with diameters less than 10 nm [1]. This novel nanomaterial has been captivating the researchers’ interests since its serendipitous discovery in 2004 through purifying single-walled carbon nanotube [2]. Due to the bonding ability of surface functional groups (e.g., carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino, carbonyl, epoxy, etc.), CDs show great potential in pollutant monitoring, [3] medical bioimaging, [4] and catalysis [5]. CDs have the advantages of abundant precursors, low toxicity, good stability and simple synthesis process. And, it also gradually replace other materials such as semiconductor quantum dots, organic fluorescent dyes and up-conversion nanomaterials [6,7]. A series of fluorescent CDs have been reported using different carbon sources, which also attract extensive interest from researchers [8,9]. In addition, CDs have the characteristic of tunable photoluminescence, thus it can be used for the practical detection of hazardous substances such as antibiotics, heavy metal ions, and others [10–13]. Those attractive properties make CDs an ideal candidate for a new generation of fluorescent nanomaterials [14].

With the progress of society and the development of science and technology, water pollution is a growing problem. And a large number of sewage has been discharged into natural water. European Union (EU) strictly stipulates a novel emerging pollutants of Pharmaceutical and Personal Care Products (PPCPs), which mainly consist of antibiotics, acyeterion, and tint, etc [15]. Among them, tetracycline (TC) is mainly used in the livestock, aquaculture industries and medical treatment. It also plays a significant role in bolstering the medicine-based development and socioeconomic progress [16]. As wide-spectrum antibiotics, TC has become one of the main pollutants in water. The abuse or related residues of TC will be accumulated through the food-chain. This ultimately leads to hepatotoxicity, nausea, side effects and poses serious hazards to human health and environment ecology [17–19]. Therefore, it is urgent to find a protocol for consecutive detection and high-efficient degradation of TC [20,21]. Conventional methods for the detection of TC include electrochemical methods, high-performance liquid chromatography, photoelectrochemical methods and fluorometry, etc. Among those, fluorometry is widely used for the detection of pollutants due to fast response, high sensitivity and low limit of detection (LOD). Unfortunately, the studies and treatments of such problems have been carried out independently, and it can only achieve mono-functional detection or degradation. For example, Liu et al. reported a recyclable fluorescent metal organic framework (MOF, BUT-179), which can be used to detect TC contents in food [22]. Hu et al. prepared a novel Ti2C quantum dots. Based on the strong interaction between the functional groups on the surface of the quantum dots and tetracycline, the sensitive detection of TC was realized by fluorescence enhancement [23]. In addition, TiO2-based geopolymer microspheres (TiO2@GMs) were successfully prepared by Dong et al. The degradation efficiency of TC still remained about 90.6 % after five reuses [24]. Wang et al. coupled a transient spark gas–liquid discharge with TiO2 photocatalysis, enhancing the TC degradation efficiency up to 90.2 % [25]. The step-by-step process is cumbersome and expensive. Therefore, the design and preparation of novel multi-functional materials capable of simultaneous detection and degradation of TC is one of the main strategies to efficiently solve the problem of water pollution. However, this is still a difficult problem to be solved. Expectantly, photocatalytic technology provides a new path for the environmental management of water pollution. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is a non-toxic and chemically stable semiconductor material. Due to its superior good photocatalytic degradation activity in the UV-region, it has been a hot research topic in the field of efficient photocatalysis [26]. However, the demerits of low band gap and easy recombination of photogenerated electron pairs hinder their practical applications [26]. The CDs have sufficient energy gap, electron storage and carrier separation capacity. Satisfactorily, the modification of TiO2 by loading CDs can effectively compensate for this deficiency and thus improve the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalysts [27]. In addition, the CDs are a typical antibiotic indicator. The CDs can be coordinate with antibiotics to form complexes with a significant change in fluorescence color, which in turn can be used for qualitative and quantitative analysis of antibiotics [27]. Based on the fascinating characteristic, the consecutive detection and photocatalytic degradation of TC by the CDs and CDs/TiO2 is highly desirable.

Herein, the CDs with salient quantum yield (QY) of 51.9 % and CDs/TiO2 were successfully developed by hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). Interestingly, consecutive ultrasensitive detection within only 40 s and almost 100 % degradation of TC was realized for the first time. CDs were applied for TC detection in real water samples with high selectivity and sensitivity. And the fabrication of the POCT platform facilitated the universality and portability of the detection, which significantly improved the detection efficiency and reduced the detection cost. Furthermore, the CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite exhibited a degradation efficiency approximately 100 % in the visible region, remarkably improving the photocatalytic activity of CDs/TiO2. In a word, the proposed protocol provides a new strategy for the consecutive on-site detection and 100 % degradation of TC in water samples for the first time, completely eradicating the pollutant hazards and displaying a prosperous application prospect.

All reagents were used directly without any purification. Citric acid (CA, 98.0 %) was purchased from Guangfu Technology Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China,). Ascorbic acid (AA, 99.0 %), cysteine (Cys, 99.0 %), PbCl2 (99.99 %), HgCl2 (99.5 %), MnCl2 (98.0 %) and CrCl3·6H2O (98.0 %) was supplied by Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Tetracycline (TC, 98 %), benzoquinone (BQ, 99.0 %), isopropanol (IPA, 99.0 %) and EDTA-2Na (99.0 %) was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). NaCl (98.0 %) was acquired from Xinbote Chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Glutamic acid (98.0 %) was offered by China Huishi Biochemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). CuSO4·5H2O (99 %), Al2(SO4)3·18H2O (90 %), and Glucose (99 %) was provided by Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). Ca(NO3)2·4H2O (98.5 %) was obtained from Beijing Yili Fine Chemicals Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Ba(NO3)2 (99.5 %) was purchased from Experimental Factory of Shanghai Chemical Engineering College (Shanghai, China). Ethylene Diamine Tetraacetic Acid (EDTA, 99.0 %), Mg(NO3)2·6H2O (98.0 %), and ethanediamine (98 %) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The ultra-pure water used throughout all the works was produced by the Milli-Q system (Millipore, Szechwan, China).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging was obtained using a JSM-7001F (JEOL, Japan) operating at 200 kV. X-ray powder diffractometry (XRD, D-Max 2550) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Kratos AXIS ULTRA DLD, UK) were employed to characterize the crystal structure and element composition of the materials. Raman spectroscopic measurements were collected using LabRAM HR Evolution. FT-IR (Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy) was obtained by a JASCOFT/IR-420 FTIR Spectrometer (Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded by a Hitachi U-4100 UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer. The fluorescent spectra, fluorescence decay spectra, and QY were obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer (Jobin Yvon FluoroMax-4). Zeta potential measurements were carried out with a particle size analyzer (Zeta sizer Nano ZS90, Malvern Ltd, UK). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were obtained with UV–vis-NIR (Hitachi). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi) was carried out to acquire the morphologies of the CDs/TiO2. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurement was done by a specific surface area analyzer (Micromeritics, ASAP2020HD88). All electrochemical measurements were performed on the CHI660E electrochemical workstation in 0.25 M Na2SO4 electrolyte with a standard three-electrode system. Among the three-electrode system, the photocatalyst-coated ITO, Pt plate and a saturated calomel electrode acted as the working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The working electrodes were prepared by following steps: The catalyst power (2 mg) was mixed with ethanol (1 mL) and Nafion (10 μL) dispersion solution and sonicated for 30 min. Then the resulting mixture (200 μL) was dripped evenly onto the bottom 1 × 2 cm2 area of the ITO glass substrate electrode surface and dried at room temperature. For electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent measurements, the measurement data was carried out at 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl electrode. over a frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 1.0 M Hz.

The CDs were synthesized by a modified one-step hydrothermal with improvement [28]. CA (1 g) was mixed with ethylenediamine (350 μL) and ultrapure H2O (10 mL) in a Teflon autoclave (25 mL) to heat at 160 °C for 6 h. The obtained deep-brown product was centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 5 min. Then, the mixture was purified through a 0.22 µm syringe membrane and further dialyzed for 24 h (MWCO 1000). Eventually, the pale-brown CDs solution was obtained. In order to manufacture high-quality fluorescent CDs, the effects of reaction time, re-fluxed temperature and reactant molar ratio were performed.

In detail, TiO2 powder (P25, 0.2 g) was dispersed into aqueous solution (35 mL) with a certain volume of CDs (1, 10, 70, 100, 150 μL). The mixture was ultrasound for 15 min and then vigorously stirred for 1 h. The above-mentioned solution was poured into polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 160 °C for 8 h. The liquid products were centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 5 min, and washed for 5 times with ultrapure water and absolute ethanol, respectively. The obtained solid samples were dried at 80 °C overnight to obtain pale-brown powder (CDs/TiO2) (The CDs/TiO2 composite added with x μL CDs was named x μL CDs/TiO2).

Fluorescence analysis was performed using a Jobin Yvon FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The quartz cuvette slit was 2 mm. For TC quantitive measurement, the CDs (100 μL) were initially mixed with ultrapure water (200 μL), and then immersed into TC solution (300 μL) with different concentrations (0.0, 1.0, 2.5, 5, 10, 15, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150, 250 μM) for 40 s. The limit of detection (LOD) was evaluated by the equation 3σ/k. Where the σ is the relative standard deviation (RSD) and k corresponds to the slope of linear equation curve. Further, selectivity and competitive experiments were investigated in the presence of other interfering sources (Al3+, Mn2+, Na+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Glu, AA, Cys and GLU), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual assay of TC on CDs-assisted smartphone-based POCT platform
ce:section.ce:para: The TC test circular filter paper (diam. 1 cm) was made with non-fluorescent filter paper. The CDs (100 μL) were thoroughly mixed with TC (300 μL) in different concentration and deionized water (300 μL). Then, paper strips were immersed into the mixed solution (600 μL) for 5 min and dried in air. The fluorescence changes of the test paper can be observed with the naked eyes and recorded by the smartphone camera under 365 nm UV-lamp. Subsequently, the color information of the filter paper was converted to RGB values by RGB APP for TC detection.

By adding the catalyst (20 mg) into aqueous solution of TC (50 mL, 20 mg/L), the photocatalytic behavior of the CDs/TiO2 for the degradation of TC under visible light can be estimated. The detailed procedure for photocatalytic pretreatment and test was depicted as follows. CDs/TiO2 (20 mg) was dispersed evenly in the TC solutions (50 mL, 20 mg/L). To establish an adsorption–desorption equilibrium, the suspension was gently stirred for 0.5 h in the dark before exposure to a xenon lamp. During the subsequent photocatalytic degradation, 3 mL mixture was taken out at regular intervals. Then, a UV–visible spectrophotometer was carried out to monitor the absorption peak of TC at 357 nm. In our work, photocatalysis was carried out by assembling visible light filters on the xenon lamp. Here, the filter acts as two functions as follows: (1) filtering out UV-light at wavelengths less than 420 nm; (2) transmitting visible light. With the assistance of the filter, the influence of UV light from the xenon lamp on the photocatalytic process can be excluded. Therefore, throughout our experiments, the photocatalytic process was carried out only under visible light.

The synthetic process of optical multi-mode CDs and high-efficient were illustrated in Scheme 1). The CDs with ultra-sensitive TC detection were developed by a one-step hydrothermal technique using CA as carbon source, EDA as carbon and nitrogen source. By rationally decorating CDs on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles, the CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst with 100 % degradation efficiency was prepared. It confirmed their degradation ability and denoted that the degradation behavior of the CDs/TiO2 catalyst was better than that of the commercial TiO2 (P25). To obtain excellent fluorescent CDs, the reaction system was optimized by insetting extensive experiments. In the following experiments, the synthesis of the CDs was individually estimated with the optimized conditions of molar ratio (nCA: nEDA, 1:65), reaction temperature (160 °C), and reaction time (6 h) (The detailed description was shown in the supporting information).

In order to highlight their structural properties, the morphological compositions of the CDs were analyzed by TEM measurements. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the CDs were microspherical (Fig. 1A) with a scale of mean size about 2.3 ± 0.4 nm (Fig. 1B). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset in Fig. 1A) displayed an interplanar spacing of 0.34 nm, which can be indexed to the (001) basal spacing of graphite [29]. TEM results indicated that the CDs were successfully synthesized. The crystallinity and graphitic structure of the CDs was further investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. Fig. 1C depicted a extensive broad peak at 22.80°, corresponding to the (002) plane of bulk graphite [30]. This probably resulted from the small size and controlled carbonization, and also disclosed the amorphous structure of the CDs.

Raman measurements was used to analyze the intrinsic state of carbon. As shown in Fig. 1D, the disordered structure (1336 cm−1) and graphite carbon (1577 cm−1) were matched with the D band and G band. In addition, it also corresponded to the polyaromatic sp3 (D band) and sp2 (G band) hybridized carbon framework of the graphitic carbon, respectively [31]. More precisely, the calculated ID/IG intensity of 0.39 indicated the predominance of sp2 hybridization in the carbonaceous cores, which assured the active fluorescence emission performance of the CDs.

XPS spectra were recorded to identify the surface chemical bonds, composition and structures of the CDs. As displayed in Fig. 1E, the full survey spectrum indicated the presence of the typical composition of C, O and N elements in the CDs. The related peaks perfectly corresponded to the C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s core level XPS scans, respectively, which were also endowed with higher resolution than that of full survey spectrum [14]. Four obvious peaks of the high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) situated at 289.6, 287.8, 286.1 and 284.7 eV were associated with O-C = O, C = O, C-O/C-N, and C-C bonds, respectively. And characteristic peaks in the O 1s convoluted spectrum (Fig. 1G) were primary to C-O at 533.9 eV and C = O at 531.3 eV, respectively. Apart from those, the N 1s spectrum with strong peak at 400.1 eV was indicative of C-N bond (inset of Fig. 1G). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) experiment was then carried out to evaluate the functionality and surface state of the CDs. Fig. 1H manifested several peaks from 1000 cm−1 to 2000 cm−1 and a wide peak at ∼ 3400 cm−1, attributing to carboxylic and hydroxyl groups on the surfaces of the CDs [32,33]. A characteristic peak at 3494 cm−1 can be assigned to stretching vibrations of surface hydroxyl groups and amino groups [34,35]. The peaks at 1672 cm−1 and 1522 cm−1 were caused by the stretching vibration of C = O and deformation vibration of –NH2, respectively [36]. The absorption band at 2910 cm−1 was considered to be the asymmetric stretching vibration of –CH2 [37]. The peaks at approximately 1290 cm−1 and 1230 cm−1 can be attributed to the C-N stretching vibration [38]. FT-IR data disclosed that the surface of the CDs was covered by hydrophilic functional groups, such as –COOH, –NH2 and –OH. It also suggested its contribution to the excellent stability and solubility of the CDs in solution. Consequently, the FT-IR results confirmed the presence of functional groups on the surface of CDs, which echoed the XPS analysis.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectroscopy, decay curve and QY were inspected to appraise the photo-physical characteristics of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the CDs depicted two dominant absorption bands at 235 nm and 350 nm. The absorption at 235 nm probably appointed to the π-π* transitions of the aromatic domains in the carbonaceous core of the CDs. And the peaks at 350 nm were ascribed to the kind n-π* transitions of -C = O/–NH2 functional groups [39,40]. The CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence under excitation of 350 nm and fluorescence spectra were measured. The excitation spectra of the CDs showed a significant overlap with n-π* absorption peaks of the surface groups, which indicated that the photoluminescence process was mainly controlled by the surface functional groups of the CDs [41]. Notably, the CDs had an excitation independent performance. It can be attributed to the uniformity of the sizes and surface functional groups of CDs, and also agreed with the previous report [42]. Among those, the fluorescent emission was strongest at 350 nm excitation, with large stokes shift of 90 nm. Therefore, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were determined to be 350 nm and 440 nm, respectively, which matched the fluorescent centers (350, 440) shown in the two-dimensional (2D) mapping (Fig. 2B). The average fluorescence lifetime decay curve of the CDs was studied and optimum-fitted to 12.4 ns (Fig. 2C). The value of QY monitored at 350 nm was determined to be 51.90 % (relevant test data is presented in the Fig. S4). Compared with reported work, our proposed CDs had a higher quantum yields.

Predominant fluorescence stability was pivotal for the practical detection of the CDs. Thus, the stability was systematically explored. In reality, Fig. S5A–5D showed feasible changes in high-intensity ionic solutions, UV-lamp irradiation for 1 h, solutions with varied pH values, and storage for 6 months, respectively. These results indicated that CDs had salient tolerance to high ionic strength, acid and alkali resistance, prolonged irradiation and photobleaching. The superior optical stability provided the basis for the application of selective detection and degradation of TC in the real environments.

The sensitivity of the CDs for TC detection were assessed under optimal system. Fig. 2D displayed that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs emission at 440 nm was highly sensitive. Meanwhile, the emission of fluorescent CDs at 440 nm decreased apparently with the gradual addition of TC increased from 0.0 μM to 250.0 μM. Correspondingly, the fluorescence decrease at 440 nm brought about a pronounced variations in fluorescence color from cerulean to quenching, which probably can be caused by the binding and interaction of the surface functional groups (–COOH and –OH) with TC. Corresponding complex in turn altered the electronic structure of the CDs. Moreover, it also promoted the nonradioactive recombination of exciton through an effective electron transfer process, ultimately leading to fluorescence quenching [43]. Under the excitation of UV-lamp, fluorescent signal can drive the color change, which effectively layed the foundation for the visual detection by naked-eyes. By plotting the I0/I versus TC concentration, we proposed two good linear regression equation I0/I = 1.11 + 1.81 [TC] (R2 = 0.992) and I0/I = 0.56 + 5.28 [TC] (R2 = 0.996) in the range of 1.0–50.0 μM and 50.0–250.0 μM, respectively. The LOD was down to 5.40 nM and 10.7 nM (Fig. 2E–2G) on account of the standard protocol of the Kaiser’s definition. Impressively, compared with other TC-responsive probes (Table S1), the CDs had significant merits in detecting TC, including LOD and linear range.

It was well-known that the selectivity was also a pivotal factor in analytical techniques. After reacting with the general interference ions (Al3+, Mn2+, Na+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Hg2+) and common potential interfering substances (TC, Glu, AA, Cys, GLU) (Fig. 2H), the sensing ability of CDs for TC was evaluated by comparing the fluorescence intensity. The fluorescent emission spectra peaked and stabilized within only 40 s (kinetic performance was shown in Fig. S6), revealing a heavy affinity between the TC and CDs. TC can significantly decrease the fluorescence emission at 440 nm, while others displayed subtle effects. Furthermore, competition experiments were then conducted to assess the sensing stability, which may have an effect on the sensitivity and accuracy of detection in the actual samples. Fig. 2I depicted that TC sensing did not suffer noteworthy interference from the coexistence metal ions and active small molecules. These results strongly provided the persuasive evidence for the high selectivity of CDs for TC, thus seating the need for selective analysis of TC in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the accuracy and reliability of TC detection, recovery test was conducted. The tap water was obtained from our laboratory, and the Yanhu water was taken from Yan Lake in the South Avant-garde District of Jilin University. To remove insoluble impurities, all water samples were pretreated with a 0.22 μm syringe. The results depicted that the recoveries ranged from 95.0 to 105.0 % (Fig. 3 , Table S2), accompanied with RSD (relative standard deviation) of 0.3–4.3 %. Recovery test meant that the CDs can accurately analyze TC in spiked water samples and had great potential for applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of CDs to TC
ce:section.ce:para:
The mechanism of TC-triggered fluorescence quenching was further investigated. Fig. 4 A illustrated that a heavy absorption overlap appeared between fluorescence emission of the CDs and UV–vis absorption of TC at 320 nm-420 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs showed a negligible change before (τ0 = 12.4 ns) and after (τ = 12.3 ns) reaction with TC (Fig. 4B). Hence, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process was completely ruled out since the lifetime can not be significantly reduced during the FRET process [44]. The detailed mechanism can be further lucubrated by the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (1)):
(1)
I 0

= 1 +
K
S V

= 1 +
K q
∙

Q

Where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of CDs before and after reacting with TC. [Q] represents the concentration of TC. τ0 refers to the fluorescence decay lifetime of the CDs. Ksv corresponds to the quenching constant and Kq is quenching rate constant. Here, the Ksv value was calculated to be 8.34 × 1013 L-1 mol−1, which represented the presence of SQE (Static Quenching Effect). The lifetime of the CDs was not obviously changed in the SQE process, which was agreed with the reported work [45]. Further, the absorption spectra of the CDs and that incubation with TC were investigated. After reacting with TC, Fig. 4C portrayed that a significant absorption of the CDs at 350 nm disappeared. This probably was induced by SQE process and facilitated the generation of CDs-TC complex [43]. In addition, the surface functional groups observed in the FT-IR and XPS analysis also implied the presence of CDs-TC complexes, echoing the UV–vis absorption data [43]. The surface electronic structure of the CDs may be changed by the complexes and underwent an electron transfer (ET) process, which ultimately culminated in fluorescence quenching of the CDs (the specific sensing process was illustrated in Fig. 4E) [31,43]. EDTA was an active chelator that will compete with functional groups on the surface CDs to acquire a specific process for the reaction of TC with CDs. On account of the stronger coordination between TC and EDTA, the quenched fluorescence caused by TC was moderately covered (Fig. 4D), confirming the formation of a novel and more stable species. In other words, the TC adhered to the functional groups (–COOH and –OH) on the surface of the CDs was released, thus partial emission was recovered. It also provided a strong evidence for the occurrence of SQE. Consequently, based on the triple synergy of the IFE, SQE and ET process, the TC can be effectively recognized by the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization and rapid detection of TC
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were employed as quantitative analysis probes for simple and fast visual detection of TC. POCT platform was utilized to satisfy the requirement of the on-site detection. Fig. 5 showed that an economical and portable CDs filter paper was established. The smartphone chromaticity analysis APP “RGB” can fetch color digitizing information from the color of the filter paper irradiated by 365 nm UV-lamp. The values of red channel (R) and blue channel (B) were obtained from the images captured by smartphone. Corresponding TC concentrations were estimated by the POCT platform from the obtained values. There were two good linear regression between the R/B ratio (y) and TC concentration (x) expressed as follows: R/B = 0.15x + 0.36 (0.2–1.0 μM) and R/B = 2.47x + 0.48 (1.0–100.0 μM). The LOD of this probe were nearly 4.0 nM and 0.24 nM, manifesting that smartphone-assisted sensing platform was promising for on-site visual analysis of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of CDs/TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: In order to reduce the band gap energy and enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2, the CDs were decorated on the TiO2. The morphological features, structural composition and elemental distribution of the CDs/TiO2 were characterized by SEM, TEM and EDS measurements. As shown in Fig. S7, the SEM analysis manifested that the CDs/TiO2 catalyst displayed a spherical morphology with an average particle size of 25 nm. Caused by the low content and small size, the CDs can not be observed in the CDs/TiO2. The TEM analysis proved the presence of the CDs in CDs/TiO2. As shown in the Fig. 6 A, the large particles with side lengths of 25 nm were regarded as TiO2, and surrounding smaller particles of CDs with an average particle size were deposited on the surface of TiO2. HRTEM images revealed the two lattice spacing (Fig. 6B). The interplanar distances of 0.35 nm paralleled with (101) plane of anatase TiO2 (Fig. 6B-1) [46]. The lattice spacing of 0.34 nm corresponded to the (001) plane of the CDs, coinciding with TEM results in section 3.1 (Fig. 6B-2). The element mapping of the CDs/TiO2 proved the presence of Ti, N, C and O elements. As illustrated in Fig. 6C-6G, the EDS spectra of the CDs/TiO2 confirmed the typical elements of Ti, O, C and N uniformly distributed in the CDs/TiO2. Therefore, TEM analysis indicated that the CDs/TiO2 catalyst consisted of CDs and TiO2 nanoparticles, and the CDs were tightly anchored on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles. The CDs/TiO2 were successfully obtained.

Raman, XRD, and XPS were verified to further evaluate the successful modification of CDs on the surface of TiO2. Compared with CDs and TiO2 (P25), the XRD pattern of the CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite was consistent with that of pristine TiO2, and characteristic peak of the CDs can not be observed (Fig. 7 A). XRD patterns implied that the crystal structure of TiO2 can not be altered by the CDs due to low content, which was well consistent with previous reports [27]. Raman analysis was further employed to verify the modification of CDs on the surface of TiO2. In comparison with the Raman spectroscopy of pristine TiO2, CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite showed two additional characteristic peaks at 1565 cm−1 and 1914 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands of the CDs (Fig. 7B). Notably, compared with CDs, the Raman peaks showed a red-shifted to some extent which indicated the presence of interfacial interactions between the TiO2 and CDs. This confirmed that the CDs was successfully decorated onto TiO2 [47]. XPS measurements were carried out to investigate the chemical composition. Moreover, it also can further evaluate the successful synthesis of the CDs/TiO2 by comparing the related BE with that of the CDs. XPS full spectrum confirmed that the CDs/TiO2 mainly consist of Ti, C, N, and O constituents (Fig. 7C), confirming the presence of each element in the precursors. And, the relative peaks well corresponded to the Ti 2p, C 1s, O 1s and N 1s core level XPS scans, respectively. The high-resolution Ti 2p, C 1s, O 1s and N 1s were displayed in Fig. 6D-F, respectively. In the Ti 2p spectrum, the BE at 464.9 and 459.28 eV corresponded to Ti 2p2/3 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 7D). Compared with the XPS spectra of the CDs, the C 1s spectrum had four peaks at 283.7 eV, 285.5 eV, 281.7 eV and 289.1 eV (Fig. 7E), the O 1s spectrum revealed two peaks at 531.2 eV and 533.1 eV (Fig. 7F), and N 1s displayed one peak at 400.8 eV (inset of Fig. 7F), respectively. The slight shift of BE confirmed that the surface chemical environment may have been affected and the CDs had successfully anchored on TiO2. Hence, XPS analysis suggested a strong interaction between CDs and TiO2, resulting in the generation of CDs/TiO2 composite.

The UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) of CDs/TiO2 and CDs were shown in Fig. 8 A. By loading CDs, the absorption edges value of CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst increased from 395 nm to 455 nm, which extended the absorption range from the ultraviolet region to the visible light range. Remarkably, the energy gap of the CDs/TiO2 (2.0 eV) was lower than that of TiO2 (P25, 2.8 eV) (Fig. 8B). This further confirmed the successful decoration of CDs and their functions to increasing visible light absorption. The valence band (VB) XPS spectra calculated that the VB edge of the TiO2 was 2.93 eV, while the CDs/TiO2 composite exhibited a red-shifted to 1.91 eV (Fig. 8C). According to Eq. (3), the conduction band (CB) value of the CDs/TiO2 (ECB = -0.09 eV) was lower than that of pristine TiO2 (ECB = 0.13 eV). These data indicated that the photogenerated electrons (e-) in the CDs/TiO2 composite were more easily transferred from the VB to the CB and also more favorable to the photocatalytic degradation of TC [60].
(3)
E g
=
E
VB
-
E
CB

where Eg refers to the band gaps, EVB and ECB are the valence band and conduction band value, respectively. The data implied that the decoration of CDs reduced the band gaps and enhanced the visible absorption of TiO2. The carrier separation behavior had an significant effect on photocatalyst activity. Hence, the FL intensity, electrochemical impendance spectroscopy (EIS) and photocurrent response measurements were carried out to investigate the performance of e-/h+ recombination, charge separation and transfer. Fig. 8D-8F depicted fluorescence spectra, impedance, and photocurrent densities of the TiO2 and CDs/TiO2. The CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst exhibited lower fluorescence intensity (excited at 325 nm), stronger photocurrent density and smaller impedance radius. The lower fluorescence intensity indicated that charge recombination can be hindered by loading CDs. Further, the stronger photocurrent density and smaller impedance radius revealed a higher charge transfer and separation. The data suggested that the CDs/TiO2 catalyst had a exceptional carrier separation efficiency even with low content of the CDs, which significantly accelerated the migration and separation of photogenerated charge pairs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of CDs/TiO2 to the TC
ce:section.ce:para:
Considering that TC was one of the most refractory environmental pollutant, the photocatalytic degradation behavior of CDs/TiO2 was investigated. Here, the degradation efficiency of CDs/TiO2 decorated with different dosages of the CDs were investigated using commercial TiO2 (P25) as a reference photocatalyst. Notably, Fig. 9 A showed that the photocatalytic efficiency of 70 μL CDs/TiO2 realized the complete degradation of TC (100 %). However, the degradation efficiency of TC itself, P25, and CDs were about 4.5 %, 78.9 %, 31.7 %, respectively. The degradation results of the CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst with various dosages of CDs (1, 10, 70, 100, 150 μL) were shown in Fig. 9B. As the dosage of CDs increased from 1 μL to 100 μL, the degradation efficiency of TC increased from 78.9 % to 100 %. Subsequently, an obvious decrease appeared when the dosages of CDs increased to 150 μL. In order to understand the degradation kinetics, the pseudo-first-order kinetic model was adopted:
(2)
l n
C 0
C
= k t

where C0 and C refer to the TC concentrations before and after reaction, respectively, and k corresponds to the reaction rate constant. The degradation of TC followed pseudo-first-order kinetics. The degradation of TC by 70 μL CDs/TiO2 exhibited a superior photocatalytic behavior with a relative apparent k that was approximately 2.5 times and 9.9 times higher than that of P25 and (shown in Figs. 9D & S8, k = 0.0217 min−1) CDs (k = 0.0054 min−1). The increased CDs loading on TiO2 generated photoinduced electrons and holes, essentially producing more active oxygen species available for surface redox reactions. Nevertheless, excessive CDs (over 70 μL) will reflect and scatter the light, signifying a decrease in light transmittance, and the increase in charge recombination can result in a decreased degradation ability. Fig. 9C showed that the presence of 70 μL CDs/TiO2 caused the absorption peak of TC about 357 nm to drop sharply until it disappeared, which strongly meant that TC molecules may have been completely decomposed by the CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst.

Three variables including the TC concentration (20 mg/L, 40 mg/L, 60 mg/L, 80 mg/L), CDs/TiO2 dosages (10 mg, 20 mg, 30 mg, 40 mg and 50 mg) and pH values (2, 5, 7 and 9) were initially explored to investigate the effect of photophysical variables on the photocatalytic process. As shown in Fig. 10 A, the degradation efficiency of TC increased from 73.5 % to 100 % with the CDs/TiO2 dosage ranged from 10 mg to 20 mg. Notably, the degradation efficiency decreased to 39.7 % as CDs/TiO2 dosages increased to 50 mg. The increase of CDs/TiO2 dosages will produce more photoinduced electrons and holes, while excessive catalysts can also reflect and scatter light. As the CDs/TiO2 dosages ranged from 20 mg to 50 mg, the degradation efficiency showed an obvious decrease due to the reduced light transmittance in the TC aqueous. Hence, the optimal dosage of the CDs/TiO2 catalyst for TC degradation was 20 mg.

As shown in Fig. 10B, the degradation efficiencies were also affected by the initial TC concentration. After 70 min of visible light irradiation, the degradation efficiency reached 100 % at a TC concentration of 20 mg/L. With the further increase of TC concentration from 20 mg/L to 80 mg/L, the degradation rate decreased to 42.0 %. In reality, the catalytic sites of CDs/TiO2 can be covered with excessive TC, hence decelerating the produce of electron-hole pairs and generation of radicals. Moreover, excessive TC solutions will introduce more intermediates, and a competitive relation for catalytic sites will present between the TC and its intermediates, sharply decreasing the photocatalytic degradation.

The surface charge and interaction between the catalyst and substrate can be altered by the pH values, thus, the degradation efficiency for TC were explored with different pH values (the initial pH values of the TC solution was 5.0). As the pH values increased from 2 to 5, the TC degradation efficiencies reached 100 %, while the degradation efficiencies obviously decreased to 53.2 % at a pH of 9.0 (Fig. 10C). The electrostatic repulsion would be improved as the increased pH values, leading to a poor photocatalytic performance [48]. However, the photocatalytic behaviors also decreased when the pH values decreased to 2. This was probably because the CDs/TiO2 and TC mainly existed as cations in solutions, and TC can be repelled by CDs/TiO2 [48]. Hence, the degradation efficiency was poor with a pH of 2. Further, to evaluate the practical application value of the material, the stability of CDs/TiO2 in the photocatalytic degradation TC was investigated by XRD, Raman, and SEM. As shown in Figs. S9–S10, the results indicated that the morphology and crystal structure of the CDs/TiO2 after degradation reaction changed slightly. Therefore, the CDs/TiO2 had good stability and showed superior potential for practical application in photocatalytic degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of active species and photocatalytic mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further study the photocatalytic reaction mechanism of CDs/TiO2, radical scavenger studies were measured. Here, benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA) and EDTA-2Na were regarded as the ·O2 –, ·OH and h+ scavengers, respectively. As shown in Fig. 11 , the degradation efficiency of TC decreased sharply (from 100 % to 14 %) with the addition of BQ. It suggested that ·O2 – was the significant reactive specie for the photocatalytic degradation. On the contrary, the degradation behaviors displayed a relatively slight decrease in the presence of IPA and EDTA-2Na, revealing that ·OH and h+ were not main active species for the degradation of TC. Hence, it can be concluded that the main active species for the photocatalytic degradation of TC was ·O2 –.

Based on the above-mentioned results and discussion, the possible photocatalytic mechanism and corresponding scheme was proposed (Scheme 2 ). Briefly, CDs played key roles in the enhanced degradation activity of the CDs/TiO2 as follows: (1) electron traps; (2) photosensitizer. On the one hand, CDs can act as electron reservoirs to gather photoinduced e- from conduction band of the TiO2 and transfer e- more efficiently to the surface, hindering the effective recombination of e-/h+ pairs. The photoinduced e- captured on the surface of CDs and in CB of TiO2 would react directly with the adsorbed O2 to generate ·O2 – and subsequently degrade TC. The h+ in VB can react with H2O or OH– to generate ·OH and degraded TC. On the other hand, the presence of the CDs improved the visible light absorbance of the CDs/TiO2, making it possible to visible light-driven degradation of TC. The band gap of the CDs/TiO2 (2.0 eV) was lower than pristine TiO2 (2.8 eV), improving mass transfer and the capability to absorb visible light. Moreover, a large number of functional groups on the surface of the CDs can be regarded as dispersion support to inhibit the aggregation of TiO2, and improved the photocatalytic activity of the CDs/TiO2 [49].

Further, in order to evaluate the possible degradation products of TC, the LC-MS was analyzed (Fig. S11). As illustrated in Scheme 3 , two possible degradation paths can be proposed based on LC-MS measurements and related researches [50–59]. For route Ⅰ, the TC molecule was attacked by ·OH and the cycloaddition reaction occurred to generate the P1 (m/z = 459.1) [50]. Then, the hydroxyl rearrangement, hydroxylation and amino group losing occurred on the P1 (m/z = 459.1), leading to the formation of P3 (m/z = 442.1). The P3, m/z = 442.1 can be further degraded with reactive oxygen species (ROS) oxidation to produce small molecule P5 (m/z = 274.3) [51]. For pathway Ⅱ,the original TC was initially stripped off hydroxyl to form P2 (m/z = 427.2) and then deaminated to transform into P4 (m/z = 410.1) [52]. Subsequently, the right half of P4 was broken and the ring structure was broken. Then dealkylation, dehydration and degraded by ·OH reactions occurred, resulting in a compound with P6 (m/z = 318.3) [53]. Then P6 was further degraded into P7 (m/z = 279.1) through demethylation [54]. which underwent cleavage of carboatomic ring A in the next step to generate P8 (m/z = 191) [55]. Moreover, the products can be mineralized successfully in these possible degradation routes (P9 (m/z = 142.0), P10 (186.0), P11 (160.0), P12 (114.0), P13 (98.0)) [56–59]. Finally, all degradation products of TC can be converted to the simple molecule (CO2, H2O, NO3 –). In addition, total organic carbon (TOC) removal efficiencies within 70 min were investigated. The TOC removal reached almost 50.1 % after 70 min degradation (Fig. S12). These result indicated that TC and its photocatalytic degradation intermediates can be finally mineralized to CO2 and H2O, and also demonstrated the predominant potential of CDs/TiO2 catalyst in the practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CDs/TiO2 catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: As reported, the photocatalytic behaviors can be effected by surface area, crystallinity, light absorption ability and efficiency of charge separation and transport [61]. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis was firstly done to compare the surface area and surface-active sites between TiO2 and CDs/TiO2, identifying the specific role of CDs in CDs/TiO2. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption curves showed that the surface area of CDs/TiO2 and TiO2 were 55.60 m2/g and 54.56 m2/g, respectively (Fig. S13). Similarly, the average pore size and pore volume of CDs/TiO2 and TiO2 did not showed remarkable change (inset of Fig. S13). For photocatalytic reactions, photocatalysts with large surface area commonly exhibited a higher photocatalytic performance due to their abundant surface-active sites for the adsorption of TC molecules. Hence, BET results showed that the enhanced photocatalytic activity of CDs/TiO2 was not related the surface area. CDs almost can not provide more surface-active sites for the photocatalytic degradation, which was also confirmed by the adsorption curves of pure CDs for TC degradation at the same time (Fig. S14). As we discussed in section 3.7, the XRD and Raman diagrams of CDs/TiO2 and TiO2 revealed that the crystallinity and loading CDs on the TiO2 were also not directly related to the improved photocatalytic activity of CDs/TiO2 catalyst. Notably, the modification of CDs on the TiO2 remarkably improve the photocatalytic efficiencies, indicating that CDs played a significant role in the increased degradation performances. The CDs contributed to enhancing degradation efficiencies in three routes as follows. (1) increasing visible light absorption (extended from 395 nm to 455 nm) and reducing the bandgap energy (2.8 eV to 2.0 eV); (2) acting as electron reservoirs and hindering the effective recombination of e-/h+ pairs for improving TC degradation; (3) accelerating the carrier transfer efficiency (corresponding discussion was obtained from the smaller impedance radius and stronger photocurrent response for CDs/TiO2 than TiO2 in section 3.8).

In this work, the multi-mode CDs with high QY (51.6 %) and excellent optical stability were successfully exploited for consecutive detection and degradation of the TC. Ultra-sensitive detection can be rapidly achieved within 40 s, with LOD down to 5.2 nM and 10.7 nM (linear intervals: 1.0–50.0 μM, 50.0–250.0 μM), respectively. Moreover, the spiked recoveries in real water samples were 95.0–105.0 % with RSD of 0.3–4.3 %. The constructed POCT sensing platform enhanced the portability and operability of the sensing system for real-time and on-site detection. In addition, the CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite increased the TC degradation efficiency to 100 % within 70 min under visible light. This conspicuously improved the photocatalytic activity of the TiO2 and complete degradation of the TC was achieved. The TC-triggered “Turn-Off” process of the CDs originated from the synergistic effect of SQE, IFE and ET. In addition, the modification of the CDs reduced the band gap of CDs/TiO2 and enhanced the visible light absorption ability of the composites. The proposed multi-functional material realized ultra-sensitive detection and 100 % degradation of TC, which eliminated the problems that cannot be solved by mono-functional materials and completely avoided the hazards of pollutants. Although possessing the above advantages, photocatalytic degradation still faces deficiencies such as high economic costs and limited engineering implementation. A majority of efforts will be made to capitalize on these advantages and address the high cost and difficulties in engineering implementation, which ultimately can achieve sustainable applications in the future.
